content.text: Why GenAI Portfolios Need Stage Gates

Stage gates are structured decision points where a portfolio review board evaluates an initiative's progress against pre-defined criteria and decides: advance to the next stage, hold for remediation, park (defer), or kill. Without them, GenAI portfolios accumulate zombie projects — initiatives that consumed their PoC budget, produced ambiguous results, and were never formally stopped, leaving teams in limbo and compute budgets silently drained.
A stage gate is not a progress report. It is a binary or three-way investment decision — go, hold, or kill — made against evidence-based criteria before the next tranche of funding is released.
GenAI initiatives have three characteristics that make stage gates more important than for conventional software:
— a model may appear to work at 100-sample PoC scale and fail at 10,000-sample production scale. Gates force measurement at each scale inflection.
Regulatory exposure that increases with scale
— a GenAI feature that attracts AI Act or GDPR scrutiny once deployed to production cannot be easily recalled. Gate 2 (Pilot) is the last low-cost opportunity to catch compliance issues.
— inference costs grow with traffic. Advancing an inefficient model to Stage 3 (Scale) can multiply operating costs 50× before the problem surfaces in billing.

The funnel narrows at each gate because the investment per initiative increases significantly at each stage transition. Gate 0 costs only analyst time; Gate 3 (Scale) commits production infrastructure, support staffing, and compliance overhead.

Each gate uses a scorecard. The board votes on each criterion as Pass, Marginal, or Fail. An initiative must pass all mandatory criteria and score above a minimum threshold on the weighted composite to advance. Failing one mandatory criterion is a kill or hold regardless of composite score.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class CriterionScore(Enum):
    PASS = 2
    MARGINAL = 1
    FAIL = 0


@dataclass
class GateCriterion:
    name: str
    description: str
    weight: float          # 0–1, sum across criteria = 1.0
    mandatory: bool        # fail = automatic kill/hold if True
    score: Optional[CriterionScore] = None
    evidence: str = ""

    def weighted_score(self) -> float:
        if self.score is None:
            return 0.0
        return self.score.value * self.weight


@dataclass
class StageGateDecision:
    stage: int
    initiative_name: str
    criteria: list[GateCriterion] = field(default_factory=list)

    def add_criterion(self, criterion: GateCriterion) -> None:
        self.criteria.append(criterion)

    def composite_score(self) -> float:
        """Weighted score out of 2.0 (all PASS)."""
        return sum(c.weighted_score() for c in self.criteria)

    def mandatory_failures(self) -> list[str]:
        return [
            c.name for c in self.criteria
            if c.mandatory and c.score == CriterionScore.FAIL
        ]

    def decision(self, pass_threshold: float = 1.4) -> str:
        if self.mandatory_failures():
            return f"KILL/HOLD — mandatory fail: {', '.join(self.mandatory_failures())}"
        score = self.composite_score()
        if score >= pass_threshold:
            return f"ADVANCE — composite {score:.2f}/2.00"
        return f"HOLD — composite {score:.2f}/2.00 below threshold {pass_threshold}"


# Gate 1 scorecard for a document-summarisation initiative
gate1 = StageGateDecision(stage=1, initiative_name="Legal Doc Summariser")

gate1.add_criterion(GateCriterion(
    name="Eval threshold",
    description="ROUGE-L ≥ 0.40 on held-out 500-doc set",
    weight=0.30,
    mandatory=True,
    score=CriterionScore.PASS,
    evidence="ROUGE-L = 0.43 on eval set v2.1",
))
gate1.add_criterion(GateCriterion(
    name="Cost model approved",
    description="Projected per-query cost ≤ $0.012 at pilot scale",
    weight=0.25,
    mandatory=True,
    score=CriterionScore.PASS,
    evidence="GPT-4o-mini at 4k avg tokens → $0.008/query",
))
gate1.add_criterion(GateCriterion(
    name="Data lineage documented",
    description="All training/eval data sources logged and cleared for use",
    weight=0.20,
    mandatory=True,
    score=CriterionScore.MARGINAL,
    evidence="Internal corpus cleared; third-party corpus pending legal review",
))
gate1.add_criterion(GateCriterion(
    name="Team capability",
    description="ART has at least 2 ML engineers with LLM production experience",
    weight=0.15,
    mandatory=False,
    score=CriterionScore.PASS,
    evidence="3 ML engineers with prior LLM deployment on ART",
))
gate1.add_criterion(GateCriterion(
    name="Business sponsor confirmed",
    description="Named VP sponsor with approved budget for Stage 2",
    weight=0.10,
    mandatory=False,
    score=CriterionScore.PASS,
    evidence="VP Legal confirmed; $120k Stage 2 budget in plan",
))

print(gate1.decision())
mandatory_fails = gate1.mandatory_failures()
if mandatory_fails:
    print(f"  Mandatory failures: {mandatory_fails}")
Strategic fit confirmed; data asset exists or is acquirable; no regulatory pre-emption
Business case rough order of magnitude; team availability; existing model landscape scan
Eval metric threshold met on PoC; cost-per-query within budget envelope; data lineage documented
Latency SLA achievable; compliance pre-assessment clear; team has production ML experience
Business KPI lift statistically significant; compliance sign-off; production SLA met on pilot traffic
NPS or user adoption above floor; ops runbook in place; model monitoring live
Benefit realisation baseline set; ROI projection updated with actuals; no open P1 incidents
Team transitioned to run-and-maintain; model refresh cadence defined
The Kill Decision: Stopping Without Blame

The hardest gate decision is Kill, and most organisations execute it too rarely and too late. By the time a GenAI initiative is killed, the team has typically spent 40–60% more than an early kill would have cost. Two patterns make early kills more likely:

Before approving an initiative for Stage 1, the gate board runs a ten-minute pre-mortem: assume the initiative is killed at Gate 1 — what would the most likely reason be? Document the top three reasons. These become the monitored signals for the Feasibility stage. If two of three signals are trending negative by the midpoint of Stage 1, the initiative is a candidate for early kill.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum


class SignalTrend(Enum):
    POSITIVE = "positive"
    NEUTRAL = "neutral"
    NEGATIVE = "negative"


@dataclass
class PreMortemSignal:
    name: str
    failure_hypothesis: str   # "we get killed at Gate 1 because..."
    current_trend: SignalTrend
    last_updated_sprint: int

    def is_warning(self) -> bool:
        return self.current_trend == SignalTrend.NEGATIVE


@dataclass
class InitiativeHealthCheck:
    initiative_name: str
    current_stage: int
    signals: list[PreMortemSignal]

    def warning_count(self) -> int:
        return sum(1 for s in self.signals if s.is_warning())

    def recommend_early_kill(self) -> bool:
        """Recommend early kill if ≥ 2 of 3 pre-mortem signals are negative."""
        return len(self.signals) >= 3 and self.warning_count() >= 2

    def status(self) -> str:
        wc = self.warning_count()
        total = len(self.signals)
        if self.recommend_early_kill():
            return f"EARLY KILL CANDIDATE — {wc}/{total} pre-mortem signals negative"
        return f"CONTINUE — {wc}/{total} signals negative"


health = InitiativeHealthCheck(
    initiative_name="Legal Doc Summariser",
    current_stage=1,
    signals=[
        PreMortemSignal(
            name="Eval quality",
            failure_hypothesis="Model cannot reach ROUGE-L ≥ 0.40 on legal domain",
            current_trend=SignalTrend.POSITIVE,
            last_updated_sprint=2,
        ),
        PreMortemSignal(
            name="Data lineage",
            failure_hypothesis="Third-party corpus cannot be cleared by Legal before Gate 1",
            current_trend=SignalTrend.NEGATIVE,
            last_updated_sprint=2,
        ),
        PreMortemSignal(
            name="Cost model",
            failure_hypothesis="Per-query cost exceeds $0.012 at realistic token lengths",
            current_trend=SignalTrend.NEGATIVE,
            last_updated_sprint=2,
        ),
    ],
)
print(health.status())

Release funding in tranches aligned to gate decisions, not in a single annual budget approval. A GenAI initiative approved at Gate 0 receives Stage 1 funding only. Stage 2 funding is conditional on Gate 1 passing. This makes the cost of a kill proportional to the stage at which it occurs and removes the sunk-cost dynamic that keeps zombie projects alive.
Gate reviews are not status updates. They are structured investment decisions with a fixed agenda:
— the initiative lead presents the scorecard with evidence for each criterion. No narrative beyond what the evidence shows.
— each board member scores each criterion independently, then votes are revealed simultaneously to avoid anchoring. Disagreements are discussed briefly; the chair breaks ties.
— composite score is computed. Mandatory failures are noted. Decision is Advance, Hold (with specific remediation actions and re-gate date), or Kill. Decision is recorded in the investment register.
— if advancing, the board states any watch conditions that will trigger an unscheduled gate review before the next planned gate.

The meeting should take 30–45 minutes. If it regularly exceeds 90 minutes, the evidence pack is not structured clearly enough or the board is relitigating strategic decisions that should have been resolved at Gate 0.
Criteria set after evidence is known

— the board sees the PoC results, then defines passing criteria that the initiative happens to meet. Fix: mandatory criteria must be defined and locked at the start of each stage, not at the gate review.
Hold used as a soft kill

— an initiative is put on Hold with a remediation plan that is never resourced. The initiative sits in purgatory, consuming small amounts of budget indefinitely. Fix: Hold decisions must include a funded remediation plan and a hard re-gate date no more than 8 weeks out. If the re-gate date passes without action, it automatically converts to Kill.
Compliance treated as a Gate 3 problem

— teams discover at scale that their model's outputs require an AI Act conformity assessment that was not planned. Fix: Gate 0 must include a regulatory pre-assessment; Gate 2 must include a compliance sign-off as a mandatory criterion.
Eval metrics gamed to pass gates

— teams tune on the gate eval set rather than a separate held-out set. Fix: the gate eval set must be a different sample from the development eval set, held by the platform or data team rather than the initiative team.

The investment register is the source of truth for all stage-gate decisions. It records each initiative's current stage, last gate decision, next scheduled gate, approved tranche, cumulative spend, and health signals. Maintaining it as a structured data artefact (rather than a spreadsheet narrative) enables automated health monitoring and portfolio-level reporting.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class Stage(Enum):
    IDEA = 0
    FEASIBILITY = 1
    PILOT = 2
    SCALE = 3
    SUSTAIN = 4
    KILLED = -1
    PARKED = -2


class GateOutcome(Enum):
    ADVANCE = "advance"
    HOLD = "hold"
    KILL = "kill"
    PARK = "park"


@dataclass
class GateRecord:
    gate_number: int
    decision_date: date
    outcome: GateOutcome
    composite_score: float
    mandatory_failures: list[str]
    next_gate_date: Optional[date]
    approved_tranche_gbp: float
    notes: str = ""


@dataclass
class PortfolioInitiative:
    id: str
    name: str
    business_unit: str
    current_stage: Stage
    cumulative_spend_gbp: float = 0.0
    gate_history: list[GateRecord] = field(default_factory=list)
    pre_mortem_signals: dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict)  # name → trend

    def last_gate(self) -> Optional[GateRecord]:
        return self.gate_history[-1] if self.gate_history else None

    def days_since_last_gate(self, today: date) -> Optional[int]:
        lg = self.last_gate()
        if lg is None:
            return None
        return (today - lg.decision_date).days

    def is_overdue_for_gate(self, today: date, max_days: int = 90) -> bool:
        lg = self.last_gate()
        if lg is None or lg.next_gate_date is None:
            return False
        return today > lg.next_gate_date

    def negative_signal_count(self) -> int:
        return sum(1 for v in self.pre_mortem_signals.values() if v == "negative")

    def portfolio_flag(self, today: date) -> str:
        if self.current_stage in (Stage.KILLED, Stage.PARKED):
            return "INACTIVE"
        if self.is_overdue_for_gate(today):
            return "OVERDUE-GATE"
        if self.negative_signal_count() >= 2:
            return "EARLY-KILL-CANDIDATE"
        return "OK"


# Portfolio register example
today = date(2026, 7, 1)

initiatives = [
    PortfolioInitiative(
        id="INI-001",
        name="Legal Doc Summariser",
        business_unit="Legal",
        current_stage=Stage.FEASIBILITY,
        cumulative_spend_gbp=32_000,
        gate_history=[
            GateRecord(
                gate_number=0,
                decision_date=date(2026, 5, 15),
                outcome=GateOutcome.ADVANCE,
                composite_score=1.72,
                mandatory_failures=[],
                next_gate_date=date(2026, 7, 15),
                approved_tranche_gbp=45_000,
            )
        ],
        pre_mortem_signals={
            "eval_quality": "positive",
            "data_lineage": "negative",
            "cost_model": "negative",
        },
    ),
    PortfolioInitiative(
        id="INI-002",
        name="Customer Support Bot",
        business_unit="CX",
        current_stage=Stage.PILOT,
        cumulative_spend_gbp=215_000,
        gate_history=[
            GateRecord(
                gate_number=1,
                decision_date=date(2026, 4, 1),
                outcome=GateOutcome.ADVANCE,
                composite_score=1.65,
                mandatory_failures=[],
                next_gate_date=date(2026, 8, 1),
                approved_tranche_gbp=180_000,
            )
        ],
        pre_mortem_signals={"kpi_lift": "positive", "compliance": "positive", "latency": "neutral"},
    ),
]

print("Portfolio Investment Register")
print("-" * 60)
for ini in initiatives:
    flag = ini.portfolio_flag(today)
    lg = ini.last_gate()
    print(
        f"  [{ini.id}] {ini.name} | Stage: {ini.current_stage.name} | "
        f"Spend: £{ini.cumulative_spend_gbp:,.0f} | Flag: {flag}"
    )
    if flag == "EARLY-KILL-CANDIDATE":
        print(f"    ⚠ {ini.negative_signal_count()} pre-mortem signals negative")

The register enables a simple automation: a daily job scans for initiatives whose next gate date has passed without a recorded gate decision, and sends an alert to the portfolio manager. This prevents the most common stage-gate failure — the gate that was scheduled but never held because everyone was busy.
Adapting Stage Gates for Agile Delivery Cadences

Stage gates originate in waterfall programme management, where each stage maps to a project phase. In an agile context where teams work in two-week sprints, the gate cadence must be reconciled with the sprint rhythm without reverting to a plan-driven delivery model.
The most practical alignment in a SAFe context is to schedule stage gates at PI boundaries. This means:
Gate 0 occurs before the first PI Planning event — the board approves Stage 1 funding for the upcoming PI.
Gate 1 occurs at the end of PI 1 (after 8–10 weeks of feasibility work) — the board reviews PoC evidence and approves Stage 2 funding.
Gate 2 occurs at the end of PI 2 or PI 3, depending on pilot complexity.
Gate 3 (Scale) may span multiple PIs if regulatory review is required.

This alignment means the gate review receives a body of evidence from a full PI's worth of work — sprint reviews, velocity data, eval run results — rather than a snapshot from a point in time that may not be representative.
Lightweight Gate Reviews for Smaller Initiatives
Not every GenAI initiative warrants a formal investment committee review. A tiered review structure based on total initiative budget reduces overhead for smaller experiments:
Async scorecard — portfolio manager approves or escalates
30-min meeting with product lead, finance, and tech lead
Full gate review with evidence pack
Full gate review + external review if AI Act in scope

The tiered structure preserves the discipline of pre-defined criteria and tranche funding while reducing meeting overhead for low-risk experiments. The key constraint is that the async format (< £50k) still requires a completed scorecard — a narrative email does not qualify as a gate decision.

Portfolio stage gates for GenAI programmes establish four structured decision points — Feasibility, Pilot, Scale, and Sustain — where a board evaluates evidence against pre-defined criteria and decides whether to advance, hold, or kill an initiative. The key disciplines are:
Set mandatory and weighted criteria before each stage begins, not at the gate review.
Fund in tranches conditional on gate outcomes — never release multi-stage budgets at the programme start.
Run pre-mortems at Gate 0 to identify the three most likely failure reasons, then monitor those signals actively through the stage.
Enforce the Kill decision when mandatory criteria fail — Hold is only valid with a funded remediation plan and a hard re-gate date.
Treat compliance as a Gate 0 and Gate 2 mandatory criterion, not a final-stage check.
These practices prevent the zombie-project accumulation and late-stage compliance surprises that are the two most common causes of GenAI portfolio underperformance.
